title: Things Fall Apart
author: Chinua Achebe
synopsis: The story follows Okonkwo, a respected warrior in the Igbo community of Umuofia, whose tragic flaw leads to his downfall. Set in pre-colonial Nigeria, the novel explores themes of masculinity, tradition, and the clash between Igbo and Western cultures.
context: Achebe wrote this novel as a response to Western portrayals of African societies as primitive. It is widely regarded as one of the most important works of African literature.

characters.name: Okonkwo
characters.description: A proud, ambitious warrior who fears failure. Strong but inflexible, he measures himself against his father's weaknesses.

characters.name: Ezinma
characters.description: Okonkwo's favourite daughter, strong-willed and intelligent. She is the only child who truly understands her father.

characters.name: Ikemefuna
characters.description: A young boy taken as a peace offering from another clan, raised by Okonkwo for three years.

characters.name: Nwoye
characters.description: Okonkwo's son, gentle and sensitive. His interests clash with his father's expectations of masculinity.

characters.name: Obierika
characters.description: Okonkwo's best friend, wise and moderate. He provides counsel and questions the traditions that Okonkwo blindly follows.

characters.name: Mr. Brown
characters.description: The first missionary who converts many villagers through patience and education rather than force.

themes.name: Masculinity
themes.description: The novel examines what it means to be a man in Igbo society, and the dangers of toxic masculinity.
examples: Okonkwo's fear of being seen as weak like his father
The banishment of Ikemefuna despite his love for the boy
The tension between Okonkwo and Nwoye over what it means to be a man

themes.name: Tradition vs Change
themes.description: Clash between traditional Igbo values and Christian missionary influence.
examples: The introduction of the missionaries and their religion
The breaking of the Week of Peace by Okonkwo
The conversion of Nwoye and other villagers

themes.name: Fate vs Free Will
themes.description: Whether characters control their destinies or are bound by fate.
examples: The village seer's predictions about Ikemefuna
The oracle's decree that leads to Okonkwo's exile

themes.name: The Power of Language
themes.description: The novel demonstrates the sophistication of Igbo language and oral traditions.
examples: The use of proverbs throughout the narrative

quotes.text: Among the Ibo the art of conversation is regarded very highly, and proverbs are the palm-oil with which words are eaten.
quotes.speaker: Narrator
quotes.context: Opening line, establishing the importance of language in Igbo culture

quotes.text: Okonkwo's whole life was dominated by fear, the fear of failure and of weakness.
quotes.speaker: Narrator
quotes.context: Describing Okonkwo's inner turmoil and driving motivation

quotes.text: When he walked, he walked jauntily; his head was held high like a fighting cock's.
quotes.speaker: Narrator
quotes.context: Describing how Okonkwo presents himself to the world

quotes.text: He had already chosen the color of his cloth, a color of the sky, and he had sent to the chief medicine man for medicine.
quotes.speaker: Narrator
quotes.context: About Ikemefuna's impending sacrifice, foreshadowing tragedy

quotes.text: The white man is very clever. He came quietly and peaceably with his religion. We were amused at his foolishness and allowed him to stay. Now he has won our brothers, and our clan can no longer act like one.
quotes.speaker: Obierika
quotes.context: Reflecting on the impact of colonization on Umuofia

quotes.text: Does the white man understand our custom about land?
quotes.speaker: Obierika
quotes.context: Questioning whether colonizers can truly understand Igbo culture
